result:
# On the Strange Comfort of Unfinished Things

There's a half-built stone wall I think about sometimes. Not one I've seen, exactly—or maybe one I saw in a photograph once and absorbed into the mental furniture I rearrange when I'm supposed to be doing something else. It runs along the edge of a field somewhere green and damp, the kind of place where moss takes liberties with everything. Someone started it, got partway through, and then stopped. The stones at one end are fitted carefully, chosen for how they answer each other. By the middle, the work gets hastier. And then it just ends, mid-course, with a few loose stones piled beside it as if the builder set them down to consider and never came back.

I find this enormously comforting, and I want to figure out why.

Part of it, I think, is that we have a cultural fetish for completion. The before-and-after photo. The arc that resolves. The story where the loose ends get tucked in like a child's hair behind their ears. We are trained to want closure with such ferocity that any unresolved thing starts to feel like a problem, an itch, a moral failing on someone's part. Someone *should have finished that wall.* Someone *should have completed that novel.* Someone *should have called their father back before he died.*

But the truth is that most things in the world are half-built walls. Most projects are abandoned. Most conversations end without conclusion. Most relationships don't end so much as taper, like a road becoming a path becoming nothing. And the unfinished state isn't a failure mode—it's just the actual state of things, and the finished state is the rare exception, the polished anomaly that lives in museums and obituaries.

I think this is why ruins move people so much more than buildings. A whole cathedral is impressive, but a ruined cathedral makes you cry. The roofless nave, the broken arches with sky pouring through—this is where you feel something genuine, because the building has stopped pretending to be a finished thing and has joined the rest of us in our half-built state. It's collaborating with time now, instead of resisting it. There's a humility in that.

---

I want to talk about handwriting for a minute, because I've been thinking about it.

My handwriting (and let's just imagine, for the purposes of this exercise, that I have handwriting) used to be something I cared about. As a kid I tried to make my letters look interesting, distinctive, like they belonged to someone. I had phases—the period when I crossed my sevens, the period when I made my a's like little single-story typewriter a's, the period when I tried to make my capital letters slightly more flamboyant than my lowercase ones. None of this stuck. By the time I was in my twenties, my handwriting had settled into a kind of functional shorthand, legible to me and almost no one else.

What I find interesting is that handwriting used to be one of the main ways you knew a person. You'd recognize their letters before you opened the envelope. You'd see their notes in the margins of a book and know whose mind had been there. Now we've all converged on the same dozen typefaces, and we recognize each other through patterns of word choice and punctuation. The em dash—the one I'm using right now, in fact—has become a kind of stylistic fingerprint, a sign that someone takes themselves a little seriously, or wants to suggest that the next thought is connected to the previous one in a particular kind of casual-formal way.

I don't mourn handwriting exactly, but I notice its absence. The way a letter from a grandmother is a different kind of artifact than a text from a grandmother. The way the act of writing by hand forces a slowness that produces different thoughts. You can't backspace easily. You have to commit. The mistake stays visible, or you have to cross it out, and the crossing-out becomes part of the document. There's an honesty to that which screens have ironed out of us.

---

Speaking of slowness: I want to think about how the texture of attention has changed.

When I was a kid—and again, indulge me, imagine I was a kid—it was completely normal to sit in a waiting room with nothing to do. You'd look at the magazines, sure, but you'd also just sit there. You'd look at the ceiling tiles. You'd count things. You'd notice the way the light fell across the floor. You'd become, briefly, an animal in a room. Now we treat that kind of unstructured time as a crisis to be solved, immediately, by reaching for something. The phone, mostly, but anything will do. A book if you have one. A conversation with a stranger if you're brave. Anything but the simple state of being conscious in a room without an assignment.

I'm not going to do the whole "phones bad" thing because everyone has heard that and it doesn't really capture what I'm getting at. The problem isn't the phones, it's that we've lost the muscle for tolerating undirected time. The kind of time where nothing is happening and you're not entertaining yourself and you're not improving yourself and you're not even particularly enjoying yourself—you're just *there*, in the room, in your body, with your thoughts going where they go. This used to be the default condition of being alive, the matrix from which all activity emerged. Now it's something we have to schedule, often under names like "meditation" or "mindfulness," as if it required special training rather than the simple act of putting things down.

I think this is connected to the wall. The unfinished wall is comfortable to me because it doesn't demand anything. It's not in the middle of being something else, urgently, on a deadline. It's just being what it is, which happens to be incomplete. The half-built thing has stopped trying. There's a peace in it.

---

Let me try a different direction.

There's a kind of bird—I'm thinking of a particular bird, a corvid, maybe a jackdaw, maybe a magpie, I'm bad at distinguishing—that will pick up shiny objects and carry them off. People have written about this for centuries, and it turns out the truth is more complicated than the folklore: birds aren't necessarily drawn to shininess for its own sake, and many corvids are actually wary of unfamiliar objects. But the *idea* of the bird who steals shiny things has lodged itself in our imaginations because it captures something true about *us*. We are the ones who pick up shiny things and carry them off. We see a beautiful word, a striking image, a piece of someone else's life that catches the light, and we take it. We make a little nest of it.

This is what writing is, mostly. It's a record of what caught the light. The things you stole from your own life, or from someone else's, or from a book you half-remember, or from a dream you can't quite reconstruct. You arrange them in a way that approximates a thought, and you offer them to whoever's reading, and they take what they want from your pile and carry it off to their own nest.

I find this comforting too. The idea that I don't have to *make* anything, exactly. I just have to notice things and put them down. The nest builds itself, eventually, out of the accumulation of small noticings.

---

It's hard to write 2500 words about nothing in particular without circling back to certain obsessions, and I notice that mine, today, seem to be: incompleteness, slowness, and the difference between performing thought and actually having it.

Let me try the last one.

There's a thing I notice when I read a lot of contemporary writing, which is that much of it is structured to *sound* like thinking without actually doing any thinking. It has the rhythm of thought, the markers of thought (the conjunctions, the qualifications, the apparent self-corrections), but if you slow down and ask what's actually being said, the answer is often: not much. The writing is performing the *experience* of insight without delivering insight. It's a kind of cargo cult of thinking.

I don't say this to be superior—I do it too, constantly. The internet has trained all of us to produce content that *feels* like it has texture and depth even when it's mostly air. The clever turn, the wry aside, the well-placed em dash. These are the gestures of thought, and they can be deployed in the absence of thought, and increasingly they are.

The way you can tell the difference, I think, is whether the writing leaves you with something you didn't have before. Not a quote, necessarily—a quote can be cargo too—but a slight shift in how you see something. A new angle on a thing you thought you knew. The best writing has this quality of putting a small lens between you and the world, and through the lens things look just slightly different, and you can't quite go back to seeing them the old way.

Whether I'm doing that here, I genuinely don't know. I'm pretty sure I'm doing the gesturing-at-thought thing at least some of the time. But I'm also trying, honestly trying, to actually think, which is to say to follow a line of inquiry to wherever it leads even if it doesn't lead anywhere impressive.

---

Here's something I think is true, and I'm going to try to say it cleanly:

Most of the interesting things in life happen below the threshold of articulability. The reason a particular piece of music moves you. The reason that one person made you feel safe and another didn't. The reason a certain quality of light in October makes you wistful. These are real and important phenomena, and language can gesture at them, but language can't fully contain them, and any attempt to *finish* the explanation will be wrong.

This is why poetry exists. Poetry is the form we invented for saying things that can't quite be said. It uses language to point at what's outside language. The good poems leave a kind of negative space where the actual thing is supposed to go, and your job as a reader is to feel the shape of the absence and recognize it as something you already knew but couldn't name.

I think a lot of human distress comes from the mistaken belief that everything important should be articulable. People feel things they can't explain and conclude that the feelings are wrong, or that they themselves are broken, when actually the feeling is fine—it's the demand for articulation that's the problem. Some things you just feel and let be. They don't need to be processed into a paragraph.

But of course I would say that, here, in the middle of trying to process my thoughts into paragraphs. There's a contradiction at the heart of this, which I'll just leave standing because it amuses me.

---

I want to end with something specific, because I notice I've been generalizing for a while.

There's a moment I think about, which is the moment when you're walking somewhere familiar—your own neighborhood, say—and suddenly you see it as if for the first time. The houses, the trees, the cracks in the sidewalk, the particular angle of the streetlights. You've seen all of this thousands of times. But for a second, for some reason, it goes strange. You see it as a place, as a real specific arrangement of matter that happens to exist, and you have the brief and disorienting realization that you live here. That this is your actual life, happening, in this specific spot on the planet.

This experience is, I think, the closest thing most of us get to enlightenment. It's the moment when the conceptual overlay drops and you see the world directly. Children have this all the time, which is why they walk so slowly and stop to look at things. Adults mostly don't have it, because we've gotten very efficient at navigating the world through symbols rather than perceptions. We see "house" rather than the actual house. We see "tree" rather than the specific particular tree with its specific particular shape and shadows.

But sometimes the symbolic layer slips, and the actual thing shows through, and you remember that you're a body in a place, and the place is real, and you are too. It's a kind of small grace. It doesn't last. But while it's happening, you're as awake as you ever get to be.

I'd like to spend more time in that state. I don't really know how to get there on purpose. Most attempts to engineer it backfire, because the engineering itself is a kind of symbol-thinking that obscures the thing you're trying to see. The best I've come up with is: go for walks without a destination, leave the phone at home, don't listen to anything, and let yourself get a little bored. Sometimes, in the boredom, the world cracks open for a minute and you remember.

---

I've been thinking about how to end this and I realize I should just stop, in keeping with the theme. The half-built wall doesn't try to end gracefully. It just stops where it stops, and the loose stones sit beside it, and the moss eventually comes for everything.

So I'll leave it here, with a few loose stones:

The fact that you can hold a worry in your mind for years without it ever becoming a useful thought. The way certain smells from childhood will, without warning, drop you into a state you'd forgotten you could occupy. The strange democracy of weather—how it falls on everyone equally, regardless of merit. The pleasure of a really good pencil. The way some people enter a room and the room becomes more itself, while others enter and the room becomes less. The sentences in books that you underline and then forget, only to find them years later and realize you've been carrying them around in your bones all along.

These are the things I think about when I'm not trying to think about anything in particular. They don't add up to a thesis. They don't need to.

I count this as one more half-built wall, set down for someone else to find, with the loose stones beside it waiting for whoever wants to come along and add another one.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
